data.title: Microsoft And Meta Earnings Preview: Capex Growth Looms Amid AI Investment
data.description:
Big tech companies' growing need to invest in technology infrastructure to support generative AI is continuing to raise expectations for capital expenditures in 2026. Looking at aggregate consensus figures for Meta Platforms, Alphabet, Amazon, Apple and Microsoft, capex grew by more than $45 billion in 2025, to $383 billion. In 2026, consensus estimates project the group's capex will grow by more than $100 billion to almost $500 billion. The strong growth in capex has caused some concerns, as it is projected to outpace revenue growth. So far, Apple Inc. (AAPL) has been the biggest wildcard among its peers, with comparatively low expectations for its capex, despite the company's indication that it will spend more on AI. As Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Meta Platforms Inc. (META) prepare to report quarterly earnings on Jan. 28, investors are looking to gauge how much the two companies will ramp up capex in 2026.

Microsoft

According to Visible Alpha consensus estimates, expectations for Microsoft's fiscal second-quarter 2026 total revenue have remained stable since late July 2025, driven by a resilient view of the company's core business segments. Revenue for the new Azure AI Services segment is projected to remain strong in fiscal year 2026, with a consensus estimate of $23.57 billion, up from $18.80 billion in January 2025. Expectations for this segment in the fiscal second quarter have increased by over 20% since July and nearly 25% since January 2025. Profitability is also expected to remain resilient for the company, while earnings per share expectations are more volatile.

We will be closely watching what the company says about the outlook for Azure and OpenAI, as Microsoft's fiscal year 2026 capex expectations have increased steadily since 2019. According to consensus projections, the company's capex is expected to more than double from $44.5 billion in fiscal year 2024 to $97.7 billion in fiscal year 2026.

Microsoft's stock has traded down 17.8% since its last earnings release and is up 6.9% since January 2025, underperforming the S&P 500's 13.8% return. The consensus price-to-earnings ratio for 2025 has fallen from 31x in July to 24x.

Could the Q2 release and FY 2026 outlook begin to drive outperformance in the stock?

Meta Platforms

According to Visible Alpha consensus estimates, Meta's total fourth-quarter 2025 revenue is expected to be $58.36 billion, driven by solid performance in the Family of Apps segment, especially in the US and Europe. The company's operating profit is expected to be $23.94 billion, driven by increasing profitability in the Family of Apps segment, offset by about $6.0 billion in losses for Reality Labs.

For 2026, earnings expectations for operating income from the Family of Apps have increased by over $1.0 billion to $110.08 billion, driven by higher ad revenue per daily active user in the US and internationally. Management commentary on the company's outlook in the earnings call will be key to assessing the potential direction of revisions for 2026. In addition, the projected losses from Reality Labs for 2025 remained steady and increased for 2026, driven by continued anticipated losses in the division.

In the first quarter of 2025, CEO Mark Zuckerberg highlighted that the company planned to invest in servers and data centers to support AI. Capex consensus of nearly $110 billion for full-year 2026 is a year-over-year increase of $40 billion from 2025. There are questions about whether Meta will further increase its capex.

Meta's stock has underperformed since January 2025, rising only 2.6% compared to the S&P 500's 13.8%. The consensus price-to-earnings ratio for full-year 2026 has dropped to 21x from 25x in 2025.

Will Meta be able to deliver a profit surprise in Q4 2025 and begin to outperform?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alliance Resource Partners: Harvesting Income From A Declining Coal Cycle
data.description:
Coal is a business in controlled decline, and Alliance Resource Partners, L.P. (ARLP), being in the coal business primarily, can be said to be also in controlled decline. This is an interesting situation - there is no dearth of coal in the world, people are just not kin to continue using coal due to its polluting nature. More importantly, cheaper and more efficient alternatives like natural gas have already replaced coal in most utilities. Similarly, there is nothing wrong with ARLP’s business as such from an operational standpoint - it has a strong balance sheet, it has multi-year customer contracts, it has two working mines in the Illinois Basin and Appalachia. But the company has seen the writing on the wall, so it is focusing on cost control rather than growth. It generates significant cash, part of which goes towards maintenance of the business - but a large part actually goes towards returning cash to shareholders. This is why it has a very strong ~10% dividend yield, and that is also why investors should look at ARLP as a finite-duration income instrument, not a long-term growth story. This is the key to understanding ARLP.

For a business that is “declining,” ARLP generates a good deal of cash. Their Q3 revenue figure was $571 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $186 million, while distributable cash flow was $106 million. Free cash flow was boosted by lower capex and cost discipline, and came in at $151 million.

Cash generation here is also supported by contract visibility. Thus, as of Q3, ARLP had 32.8 million tons of coal contracted and priced for 2025, and 29.1 million tons already contracted for 2026. Being largely fixed priced and not indexed, these contracts bring stable near-term cash flows for ARLP. Besides, coal also keeps serving as a reliable peak-load fuel for utilities when demand spikes.

These are strong numbers. If you look at the multiples, as well, ARLP is not cheap, nor is it too expensive. Trailing EV/EBITDA is a sober 5.5x, forward earnings is ~10x, and EV/sales is only 1.6x. On the one hand, these are not cheap, which means the market understands that the cash flows are real and coming in strong. On the other hand, they are not expensive multiples, either - which shows that the market is pricing in the terminal nature of the coal industry.

ARLP trades at a meaningful discount to sector median valuation multiples, with EV/EBITDA around 5-6x versus a sector median closer to 7-8x. That discount comes from the market’s lack of faith in coal cash flows over long durations.

You get the same sense if you look at ARLP’s distribution. Their payout ratio is extremely high - GAAP at 138%, non-GAAP at 122%. That means, they are paying out more than they are earning. On any normal dividend screen, this won’t look good. However, this is not an error. ARLP is designed in this way, to distribute cash when available. Being an MLP, or Master Limited Partnership, ARLP behaves like a terminal asset, with no interest in preserving earnings coverage.

So, for income investors, the takeaway is simply this: ARLP is a high-yield but finite duration income asset, to be used opportunistically while the coal demand lasts (at least this decade).

What Actually Funds the Distribution

ARLP has long-term coal contracts with its customers. These typically go for 2-3 years, and so we have good cash flow visibility. By Q3 2025, they already had 32.8 million tons of coal contracted and priced for 2025. Of these, 29.8 million was for domestic consumption, and 3.0 million for export. Even for 2026, 29.1 million tons were already contracted before the year began - a slight 9% increase quarter over quarter. Prices of these contracts are mostly fixed, not indexed - so that also improves visibility. According to management guidance, there is a chance Appalachia prices in 2026 will be lower than 2025 prices: legacy 2022 contracts signed at higher prices during the energy crisis are being replaced this year with new contracts. However, new cost improvement measures will offset the margin loss, according to management.

In Q3, margin retention remained solid. Cost reductions were seen in both mines owned/operated by ARLP, which led to improvement in adjusted EBITDA. Full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA expense per ton was guided to $34–36 in the Illinois Basin and $60–62 in Appalachia. These are/were economically viable while the higher-priced legacy contracts run. Costs have improved, so ARLP is able to sustain distributable cash flow despite falling coal prices. For the first nine months of 2025, operating cash flow was $507.3 million, against $218.5 million of capital expenditures. That shows ARLP is still firmly free-cash-flow positive.

However, as new contracts set in, prices will drop, and while there will be some reduction in costs due to existing infra, better cost discipline and non-core restructuring, cash flow will move from peak to harvest mode. ARLP is in decline, but while it lasts, income investors will continue to benefit.

I should note here that ARLP's cash generation does not come from coal mining alone. The MLP earns stable, high-margin cash flows from oil & gas royalties and infrastructure-related investments. These require minimal investments and act as a cushion against coal price and demand volatility.

ARLP continues to spend very little on growth. Management clearly stated in the earnings call that they have no plans to add any new mining unit. The only growth-related expense was $22.1 million invested in a PJM coal-fired power plant (Gavin-type investment). This was a purely royalty-focused investment. Besides this, for the first nine months of 2025, ARLP paid $259.4 million in distributions. This was much more than GAAP earnings of $228 million over the same period. They are not leaving anything behind for growth capex, with the entire expenditure becoming maintenance-oriented.

To come back to the original point, ARLP’s distribution is funded by contracted, near-term revenue from coal, and a much-improved cost basis. Their capex discipline also converts their entire EBITDA into cash flow. While this does not make distribution perpetual, it does guarantee a high distribution for a few more years, even as the market assigns what is essentially a terminal valuation to the MLP.

How Long Does Coal Last? A Forward-Looking View

The coal industry is rapidly declining, being replaced by less capital-intensive and less polluting industries like natural gas, solar and so on. What ARLP investors need to understand is - how rapidly? ARLP still continues to generate cash - how long will this last? - these are key investment questions.

Coal’s decline is clearly seen in capacity, which peaked at around 318 GW in 2011. By 2024, operating capacity had already fallen to ~175 GW. IEEFA estimates operating coal capacity will decline to ~115 GW by 2030 - or two-thirds of 2011 peak capacity will be retired in less than five years. We see the same decline in demand as well. By 2007, coal consumption had peaked, and fell ~60% to ~410 million short tons by 2024. Forward projections at the time also show further decline - into mid-to-high 400 million ton range by 2025–26.

Coal is still surviving because of at least two reasons: one, coal is embedded in multiple systems so replacement is a long term chore. And two, President Trump has made an admittedly belated effort to revive it. Coal is a fallback option in cases of peak demand, especially from data centers. As for Trump, this year he put a $625 million budget to revive the coal industry. This administration’s general posture is to resist policies that “steer the US away from coal.” Despite this, S&P Global says that coal production fell 26.5% during Trump’s first term, while coal-related jobs fell 18.2%.

Trump’s efforts have given the industry some hope - even if ESG groups aren’t happy. Maybe a regulatory environment more adaptive to coal may extend its lifespan beyond 2030 - at least in the developed geographies. For ARLP investors, that could mean a few more years of solid dividends. New contracts, however, have to come at good prices. The market is aware that ARLP is on borrowed time. That is why it trades at flat multiples, has an aggressive cash return strategy, and it should only be owned by investors aware of its finite life-time.

Balance Sheet: Containing Decline Risk, Not Funding Growth

As of the latest quarter, ARLP’s net debt of around $350–375 million is around half of trailing EBITDA of around ~$720–750 million. This provides ARLP with a meaningful cushion against volume and pricing decline. Liquidity is good, with cash on hand as well as the revolving senior secured $1 billion credit facility - largely undrawn - sufficient for meeting near-term obligations. This one matures in 2028, and there are no other near-term debt maturities, reducing refinancing risks.

This healthy balance sheet allows ARLP to manage the coal decline on its own terms. They do not have to defend distributions or cut them reactively, ARLP can recalibrate payouts as needed - just like they did in 2025. A gradual decline in coal demand does not break the model they have here. What would really break it - what would make the balance sheet unable to absorb demand decay - is a sudden contraction, a faster-than-expected collapse in contract volume. As we discussed system friction and Trump’s efforts to revive coal, this does not seem likely.

Material Risks

ARLP’s valuation already accounts for ESG optics and long-term decarbonization narratives. No, the real risks here are timing, speed, and contract durability.

Like I was just noting earlier, the primary risk is a faster-than-expected contraction in utility coal demand. Reasons for such a scenario could be either accelerated plant retirements or regulatory changes. There are currently no US-wide possibilities of such things happening - indeed, while the current administration lasts, coal will have its short term saviour. That, plus data center demand, will ensure that there are no surprise closures.

However, the same cannot be said about the second risk - contract repricing. ARLP saw higher priced contracts in 2022 during the energy crisis. Today, new contracts come with lower but more sustainable prices. Now, declining demand may influence customers to press for easier pricing, even if regulators today want to stave off the decline. However, if pricing weakens faster than costs - particularly in Appalachia - cash flow comes under pressure, forcing distribution distress.

Q4 2025 Earnings Outlook

ARLP will report Q4 2025 earnings on February 2. Income investors should not just focus on revenue growth, but on three other items - contract realization, cost discipline, and how well ARLP sustains distributions. Key metrics that should be watched include coal sales volume versus what was committed in contracts; realized pricing as old contracts end; and how segment-level costs are moving in both the Illinois Basin and Appalachia. We should also watch out for management guidance on 2026 contract pricing, and how they expect capex to move. These items will determine if ARLP can sustain its current $0.60 quarterly distribution through the next contracting cycle.

Conclusion: A Hold for Income, Not a Bet on Revival

It is best to look at ARLP as an investment that can provide a strong income for a limited duration. From what we discussed here, that duration could be as little as the next 3-4 years, or as much as another entire decade. Given their revenue visibility, careful investors should be able to predict this well in advance. Meanwhile, their MLP structure will continue to generate real cash from contracted volumes and cost discipline.

That makes ARLP neither mispriced nor distressed for the sole purpose for which it should be considered - income. Expect it to continue to provide a high but slightly variable income for at least the rest of this decade. That makes it a Hold for income investors. It is not a Buy for new investors because the market has already priced in the income visibility of ARLP.

data.title: Meta: The Moneymaker Without Strong AI Capitalization
data.description:
Meta (NASDAQ:META) stock has returned 9.1% since my last report outperforming the S&P 500’s gain. In my view, the company remains a one-trick pony on ads but the company is good at what it is doing augmented through injection of artificial intelligence. Meta will be reporting Q4 2025 earnings on the 28th of January. In this report, I discuss the risks and opportunities for Meta, the expectations for Q4 and I update my price target.

Meta’s Different Approach Towards Monetizing AI

Traditionally, Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) and Microsoft (MSFT) are seen as the Tier 1 global hyperscalers. Sometimes Meta appears in that list to and sometimes it does not. That, however, is not driven by their Capital Expenditures. The CapEx over the past 12 months is just 11% lower than what Microsoft has spent on capital expenditures.

Where Meta substantially differs is that it has no AI centered or front-end AI product that is currently being capitalized in a substantial way. Instead, the company leverages AI to improve ad-targeting, optimization and content discover. By doing so, the company tries to better pair ads with eyes and thereby improve ad revenue. So, the company leverages AI on their free platforms with ads.

The company does have front end applications in search bars that include AI-driven recommendations and suggestions, Meta AI Assistant and AI-driven content creation tools. Meta is playing with the idea to introduce paid plans for Meta AI Assistant, which I believe is the most promising front-end or product to capitalize on. The company can also inject paid AI agents in their social media and media platforms, which could be particularly interesting for businesses which often take appointments and orders through one of Meta’s apps.

So, Meta is not engaged in the Cloud & AI arms race that we see with Microsoft, Alphabet and Amazon but it has free platforms and AI injections that can be capitalized on.

Non-AI growth drivers could eventually be the products that come from the Reality Labs such as the virtual reality products. However, that segment is consistently loss making for Meta at this moment and I believe AI capitalization has a more promising growth path.

The Risks For Meta

I generally consider Meta’s approach to be somewhat higher risks. That is because the main AI use case right now is driving up ad-engagement by optimizing the pairing of ads and audiences. That drives up engagement and could result in Meta structurally generating higher ad revenues. That process of pairing is a constant process, but I also believe that eventually having clusters running may not weigh beneficially against the benefits of upside on ad revenues. So, to justify high AI CapEx other tools and opportunities need monetization now rather than later. We also note that AI generated content may ultimately be detrimental to social media platforms and regulatory risk may give Meta challenges to roll out products that it can capitalize on as it is clear that Meta injects AI in its products with the potential to capitalize on them later on. That is not a lock-in method that regulators are keen on. We also note that ChatGPT, Gemini and CoPilot are currently having a combined market share of around 90%. So, the trick for Meta actually will be gain market share and that can be done through leverage its Family of Apps (Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp).

We also note that several countries are working on social media bans for children, which may weaken the generational strength that Meta has built and subsequently their ability to leverage the Family of Apps for commercial purposes.

What Analysts Are Expecting For Meta In Q4 2025

For the fourth quarter, analysts expect Meta to report $58.5 billion in revenues marking 20.8% growth. The estimates range between $53.8 billion and $59.7 billion. Revenue estimates increased by 2.5% over the past six months and by 0.4% over the past three months. 41 analysts revised estimates upward, while four analysts revised estimates downward. I believe the increase is driven by higher expectations of Meta’s ad optimization strategy. The company has a perfect track record beating estimates since Q3 2022.

EPS is expected to be between $7.60 and $8.65 with a consensus estimate of $8.20 indicating 2.2% growth in earnings per share. EPS estimates saw 13 downward revisions and 28 upward revisions over the past three months leading no major swings in the expected EPS over the past three months. Over the past 12 quarters, the company missed estimates once and that was in Q4 2022.

Meta Stock Remains Attractive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Meta Platforms seems to have attractive upside based on the company’s median EV/EBITDA. I am adjusting my price target down from around $905 to $899. This is driven by free cash flow estimates having been revised downward by 5.6% through 2027 with a 1% revision in 2025, 12.2% revision in 2026 and 6.2% revision in 2027. The free cash flow is getting compressed by higher capital expenditures. On EBITDA, estimates improved by 0.5% with a 0.1% increase to the estimates in 2025, down 0.5% in 2026 and up 1.7% in 2027.

If we look at margins, we note that there is a dip expected for 2026 and I believe that is driven by the lag between revenue realization and cost of building out the AI side of the business, which doesn’t just include physical infrastructure but also AI talent costs and operating costs. Operating cash flow is set to rise substantially, but without major benefit for free cash flow as capital intensity is set to increase sharply to over 80% in 2026. However, at the same time we note that the company has an incredibly strong cash position which I believe also shows that while I am not impressed by companies that primarily capitalize on ad pairing, they are just doing it extremely well.

Conclusion: Meta’s AI Build Out Is Impressive

Meta’s approach towards AI is not necessarily a convincing one as it has no main product that it currently capitalizes on through AI. AI simply optimizes for Meta at this point. Front-end solutions for consumers are currently free and that is where the company’s strength may be. Eventually, Meta could capitalize on the large user base of the Family of Apps and by that time it also has super clusters up and running providing the company strength from the side of the user base and the compute or inference capacity to capitalize on that user base. The company does not engage in the cloud computing arms race like Microsoft and Amazon, but instead tries to couple its existing using userbase to AI first through free options and later on to paid options.
